speaker: interviewer
text: With Ruth Foster in Winchmore Hill, London, England, and this is tape number-- four?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were literally thrown into this big ship. I don't know how many of us, but quite a few hundred, or maybe thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was August-- the 6th of August, 1944. And we were in this dungeon-- like a dungeon-- and suddenly we emerged in Gdansk in Danzig. And there we were, on a big-- like, a lawn next to the-- next to the harbor. We had to assemble. And men and women were separated. And don't know what happened to the men, but the women, we went in small boats. We were transported from the big boat into small boats, and then we had to march for hours and hours without-- you must now remember-- without food, without anything to drink, with inadequate clothes, with bad shoes, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you marching for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was only a matter of hours from there, until we came to a-- in German, they said [GERMAN]-- to a forest. But they had taken all the-- all the trees away-- you know, like a big place in a forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: A clearing. They'd made a clearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: A clearing. Thank you. A clearing in the forest with watchtowers, with a big, iron gate. And it says the same thing as in Auschwitz, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- 'Work makes you free.' And underneath, it said [GERMAN]. Behind there were beautiful houses-- houses where families lived, behind these gates. And in the distance, we saw barracks. And as we came nearer, we felt this terrible smell that hung over the whole place-- as when you boil eggs and the water boils away, and then the eggs start burning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sulfur.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it gives this terrible stink. And that hung over the whole place, and we didn't know what it was. And something like rain came down-- gray, like ashes. We also had no idea what it was. And big chimneys-- three big chimneys. And out of these chimneys came the-- these ashes. We thought, it's a factory of some sort. They herded us into a shower room-- the women. Now, don't ask me how many. Quite a few of us. They herded us into the shower room. We had to undress first. We had to leave everything we had-- you know, this gray dress they gave us. And only our shoes we could take with us into the shower room. They gave us a piece of soap, and there we waited in the shower room. And no water came. And then they herded us out again the other side. They gave us striped prison garbs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you waited and the water did come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did not come. It never came. The water never came. They opened the gates on the other side of the-- of the shower room, yes, and let us go out. Later on, we saw that there not only was this one shower room, they had three or four of them next to one another, and they were gas chambers. We were in this gas chamber waiting for the water to come, thinking a shower room. See, at that time, we had never heard of-- of-- of gas chambers. So that was-- we were lucky they let us go out the other side. Then there were prisoners standing there who had positions-- some that I told you already. There was a green triangle, and they were the criminals. And the pink were the Jehovah's Witnesses, and the lilac were the homosexuals. They had usually-- they were in charge of us. And the SS women. They gave us these clothes, and we were herded into barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those were the clothes that you came in? You got the same clothes back?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the clothes I was in then until we were liberated. That is the dress there. And there we were just vegetating there, with hardly any food, with hardly any water. No sanitation. The toilet was-- was a pit with a tree trunk over. And by that time-- I don't know whether I should tell this now-- some women had already lost such a lot of weight, when they were sitting on these tree trunks to pass motion, the whole intestine-- everything was hanging out already-- I mean, terrible. We were younger. With us, it wasn't that bad, but the older ones who had still survived up till then were in terrible conditions. Every day-- or that maybe-- morning and night, we had to stand [? up here. ?] Roll call. They would count us from morning till night. And we had to stand still. Not allowed to talk, and not allowed to even move. The men were in a different camp. They had to go. They had hats up, hats down, and then we had to bend our knees for hours on end. And who couldn't go and couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: --carry up anymore, they were taken away, put at the side. We also didn't know at the time why-- where they were taken. But it didn't take long until we noticed that they had the gas chambers and the crematorium-- these chimneys were the crematorium-- and the gas chambers they had in Riga-- in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to do any work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't do any work-- any work. Every so often they had selections where we had to get undressed, and the SS would just march up and down, and selected people-- the elderly who had lost a lot of weight. Women and men were separated then. They were taken to the side. Somebody who looked old got taken to the side. Somebody who looked too young were taken to the side. I mean, it was done at random. One day, at one of these selections, I was taken out with 10 other girls. We were 10 girls, roughly all the same age, roughly all the same height and figure. We were taken to one barrack, marched away from the others to a separate barrack. And there we saw beds-- white beds with white linen, men in white overalls. Oh, I thought, maybe we can work here or-- but we were not allowed to talk and ask questions. We were marched in there, and we were given an injection. We had to undress with our bundles at our feet. We stood there straight, got an injection. And while this injection worked, I heard in the camp they went around with loud hailers-- whoever worked for the RBR-- you know, the German army-- in Riga should assemble outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to one of the men there, I said, I worked in Riga for RBR. Out! Run out! Out of here! Out of here, he said. Go! Go! So later on, years later, I thought they did make experiment there. That was [? experiment ?] block, as they called it. So you never knew what they injected into you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I never knew what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but you didn't know what-- they gave you an injection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The injection was-- my husband, who was a doctor, thought it might have been a hormone injection because I grew a lot of hair on my legs-- only on my limbs. On my legs and on my arms. Nowhere else on the body-- I mean, it's quite normal. But don't know what sort of injection it was, but I found out it was the experimental block, as the called it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were women in beds. I thought, this is good here. You get food here. You have beds, you know. But I was lucky that I got out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there for about six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp. And it went from bad to worse. They were taking out people to work. But nothing ever came from this-- from the army. The army never took any workers for themselves. It was just a fluke. And maybe it was just a voice from God or something that-- that-- that called, you know, that those who worked for the Germans in Riga should assemble, which we did. But we never worked for them anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on, 500 of us were taken out from there to work. We went-- we were taken out from there, and on cattle trains under terrific conditions-- some of us had already typhus-- to Ravensbruck. But as some of us had typhus, they didn't let us go into the camps, so we were in quarantine outside. We stayed there for a few days, and the ill ones were left behind, the sick ones, and we were transported in open cattle trucks to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the eastern zone of Germany-- Poman-- Pomeria-- East Prussia-- to East Prussia-- without food, without water. And the weather was dreadful. Till we got there, and we had to-- we were in a-- what did you call it? In the forest? In a clearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a clearing in the forest there were barracks, and we were put into these barracks. There were no floors to them-- just straw, which became wet and awful after we-- after we were in there for a while. We had to work for a German firm for a road-building contractor. His firm is still-- years ago when we went through Germany by car, his firm was still in business-- [GERMAN] and Son. They had big [? holders ?] up on the autobahn-- [GERMAN] and Son, Konigsberg. But they were then-- that was East Germany, but they were then-- by that time, they were in Western Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked there under dreadful conditions. But our commandant, a German SS man, he was very humane. He couldn't make our life easier, but he didn't make it any harder for us. He went to Stutthof, which-- we were under Stutthof, under the concentration camp Stutthof-- affiliated to it. Even so, we were shortly in Ravensbruck, but never registered there. But we were registered in Stutthof. So he went back there for getting our provisions, and then he brought us these pullovers. Everybody got a pullover. Everybody got men's long johns, underpants, and a coat. So he tried, at least. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [? Schultz ?]. His name was [? Schultz ?]. But the SS there were not as bad as the SS women. The SS women were absolutely monsters. Monsters. The SS were drunk all the time. You hardly ever saw the commandant and the other ones. But the SS women were monsters. They had these big Alsatian dogs which they let loose on us. Ever since then, I'm afraid of dogs, even if it's a little poodle. I-- I'm-- I'm scared stiff. When I go for a walk here in the park and I see a dog, people must think, oh, there's a mad woman there. I can't-- I sort of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it goes back a long way, doesn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It goes back a long way, but I should know better by now. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived under terrifically bad conditions. We had no running water-- nothing. We had to wash in a lake. The SS girls took us to the lake. And the lake by that time had frozen over, and we had to knock a hole into the lake for us. And they insisted that we undressed ourselves and washed ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's where we stayed there, until Christmas of '44, when suddenly we had to march out. We didn't know where and what. Those who had still survived were assembled, marched out of the gates accompanied by SS men and SS women and the commandant, who-- they drove in a car, but we marched and the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: And wherever we walked, however many miles we walked, we saw different groups coming-- also prisoners that worked in different places. And we all marched one direction because the Russian front was coming. The Russians were coming in. By that time, Riga was-- I heard later on-- Riga was already liberated, and the whole of Latvia was already liberated from the Germans who were pushed out. And they came then nearer. So that's why they wanted to get rid of us. So they marched us. The conditions, I can't tell you. Somebody said eventually we were-- we were 3,000. And we marched for six weeks. At night, they drove us into forest-- during the day, they drove us into forests so that people shouldn't see us. And at night, we marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you manage to sleep in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. How can you sleep? You had no food-- nothing. We just huddled together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just thought, through exhaustion.

words.text: But I just thought, through exhaustion.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said we're cold. We made an oven. We kept each other warm. We were huddled together. We looked like skeletons. We were skeletons. We looked like-- we didn't resemble human beings anymore. And that was on the 10th of December-- 10th of March, 1945. We marched for about six weeks. Later on, we were told. We had no idea what the dates were and how-- the span of time. But we marched for nearly six weeks. We set out 3,000, and we were liberated by the Russians on the 10th of March-- only 300. They drove us into a-- into a barn, into a big barn on an estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, before.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the morning of the liberation day. They drove us into-- they herded us into this barn. The SS were still guarding us. And we were about 300. Then we heard-- in the distance we heard cannons and shooting. So we thought, now they have us here in this barn, they come and shoot us. [INAUDIBLE] Our last minute has come.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened was we heard the tanks. The tanks came in onto this-- onto this farm, you know, onto this big estate. It was not an ordinary farm, it was a big estate. The Russian tanks came rolling in. But we didn't know-- was it Russians, or was it Germans? It was a fighting front outside this barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Germans fled-- the soldiers. And suddenly, there were the Russians. They were our liberators. They were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what was it? The hour of liberation. We didn't even-- we were so ill at that time. A friend of mine and I, we had typhus, but were still very afraid. We had high temperature and the most shocking diarrhea. We were afraid the Germans may come back. And it was a fighting front, which had [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we crawled away from there. But as we crawled out of this barn, the Russians had shot all our--

speaker: interviewer
text: SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. All the SS women and our commandant, as well. They were all lying there, dead. They had to assemble in a half circle. And with a machine gun, they just-- they didn't ask any question-- were you good, or were you bad or whatever. They just shot them down.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this friend of mine and I, we crawled away from there and onto the main roads. The Germans, they were fleeing from the Russians. It was chaos-- absolute chaos.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked on to the next big town. I can't tell you now how long it took. We were hiding from the Russians because they were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] were raping Jewish women, as well. My friend and I, we were lucky. We were hiding and crawling out at night. Until one night, one evening, we were coming to a big town. Later on, we knew it was Bromberg-- Bydgoszcz in Polish. And we were hiding in an entrance to a yard or something. It was a big gate, and we were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't speak a word of Polish. That was Polish territory. We were afraid of the Poles. We were afraid of the Russians. You know, we didn't know-- we were just saying-- we  were saying [INAUDIBLE] all the time, you know? We're just praying all the time. And we were sorry that we were still alive, and we were pleased that our parents didn't have to live through all this what we had lived through to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then suddenly, we heard footsteps. And they stopped in front of us, these footsteps. And we didn't dare to look. They were like marching steps. And as we looked up, there was an officer standing in front of us-- a Russian officer. I saw because he had all sorts of pips here on his shoulder-- shoulders, and in front of his helmet. And he addressed us in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]? So we said, yes. And he introduced himself. He was a chief of a big Russian military hospital in Bromberg in Bydgoszcz. And he took my friend and me to this hospital where we were nursed back to health. But the war was still on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say, all the officers, the doctors, and staff nurses-- whoever had any pips-- they were all Jewish in the Russian army. All Russian Jews we came across. They were very good to us and nursed us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until June of '45. That was from March to June, and-- because we were very weak. And we were in isolation, of course. They put us in the basement because we were infectious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take for you to get rid of the typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. Weeks. We had high temperatures. They came in-- doctors came in with medicine and food for us every day. And-- I don't know-- might have taken weeks. And then we came-- they put us in the ward of the hospital after we were not infectious anymore. And I was apparently still delirious, I was told after. And they employed German nurses-- German Christian nurses. I don't know where they came from, but they were nursing us. Not just Russian nurses, but also German nurses. And apparently, I would have said, in my delirium, [? 'Once ?] more Friday night,' like it used to be at home. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we were in the Russian hospital-- and they sort of adopted-- adopted my friend and me. They wanted to take us back to Russia. In the meantime, the war had finished. They had adopted my friend and myself. And the old-- the chief doctor, you know, they called it a [? [RUSSIAN] ?]. He was a chief. His name was [? Milan ?] [? Vladimovich ?] [? Schwatzman ?]. And he said in Yiddish, it's [? Maya ?] [? Wolf ?]. So he wanted to take us back to Russia. He came from Pryluky, a town between Odessa and Kiev in the Ukraine. So I said to my friend it was high time that we get away form here. So we explained at great length that we have to go home. We might still have surviving relatives. They were reluctant to let us go, but we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took me from June to August to come back to Lingen. Everything was-- the railways didn't go because everything was destroyed. Nothing worked. Part of the way, we-- we walked or marched with-- with French prisoners of war who also had been liberated and wanted to go back to France. So we marched with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the way, we walked on foot by ourselves. Then a Russian lorry would take us a little bit further towards the border, until we came to Berlin. Because we wanted to go to Berlin-- without documents, without anything. That's why were in these dresses. It's our identification. [LAUGHS] Stupid-- I mean, anybody could have put these dresses on, couldn't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we came to Berlin. And there's a former Jewish hospital-- was in the [GERMAN]. They made a refugee home for refugees. There I met my cousin, who had also been liberated. And she had a very bad leg, and she was nursed back to health in the [GERMAN]. But I wanted to go back to Lingen because I had a boyfriend in the Lingen ghetto. And we said if we survive, we meet in Lingen at my parents' friends. So I thought, maybe he has survived, and we might meet. So I went back to Lingen. But I found out already before I came to Lingen that he was shot. He tried to flee from Riga, being from Riga, and he was shot while escaping. I found out afterwards. But at that time-- I found out in Berlin. But at that time, I didn't believe them. I still wanted to go back to my hometown. The friend of mine with whom I was together all the time, from Stutthof till the liberation and in Bromberg, she came from Dresden. She was also, like me, an only child. Her parents were not-- she had to be deported, but her parents had to stay behind. They worked for [GERMAN] They made these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lenses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lenses. So they had [INAUDIBLE] for the war effort. They were put aside, so they were not sent on transport. But she had to go on her own, the poor girl. So she hoped that her parents might still be in Dresden. But she found out later on there was no sign of them. I wanted to go-- at that time, Berlin was only Russian. Later on, it was occupied by the four powers, but at that time, it was only Russian-occupied. Oh, things went on there-- something horrific. But anyway, they were our liberators. I mustn't say too much. But they were good to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to go into the-- I had to go into the American zone first before I came into the British zone where my hometown was-- my town of birth. Hometown, I don't want to say that, really. My place of birth. So one night, I swam through the river Mulde. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no bridge or anything. The German refugees wanted to go across a pontoon bridge, which the Russians had made. And so they were lying in the meadows, hoping that the Russians would let them go through. But I didn't want to mix with the Germans because they were all treated, the civilians, as war criminals-- you understand? Even women and children and old men-- the Germans. They wanted to get from Eastern Germany into Western Germany without any luggage. They also left everything behind. But I didn't want to mix with them. I thought to myself, I don't want to identify with them. Why should I identify with Germans? So I took it upon myself. One night, I swam through the river. I thought to myself, what can I do? And the Russians were patrolling the bridge with a rifle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took a moment, and I swam through the river. Don't ask me how wide it was. It was fairly wide. But I swam through it in my-- I took that dress off, and I had a little bag in the meantime. Put that into my-- into the bag, put that on my back, and I swam through the river. And I came to a farmhouse that was American zone. And I knocked at the door, and I explained who I was. And they took me in, gave me hot milk and hot blankets and they dried me and so on. And then on foot and on railways, which were going-- some railways still worked. You know, most of the tracks had been bombed. I was trying to go always a little bit further, little bit further, little bit further, until the 22nd of August, I came to my hometown, Lingen.
